intent: Define the SecurityRecipes MCP elicitation boundary so agent hosts, MCP clients, gateways, and enterprise buyers can decide when a server may ask a user for form input or direct the user to an external URL without leaking credentials, tokens, payment data, or prompt-injection-controlled context through the LLM, client, or intermediate MCP servers.

positioning.buyer: AI Platform, Product Security, IAM, Privacy, GRC, procurement security, and enterprise architecture teams
positioning.category: MCP elicitation, URL-mode consent, and sensitive interaction boundary
positioning.enterprise_promise: SecurityRecipes can prove that human input requests from MCP servers are identity-bound, mode-aware, consented, URL-safe, and logged before user data or third-party authorization enters the agentic workflow.
positioning.why_now: MCP 2025-11-25 introduced URL-mode elicitation for sensitive flows that must not pass through the MCP client. That is an enterprise-grade feature, but it creates a new trust boundary: users can be phished, forms can request secrets, URLs can carry sensitive data, and servers can confuse external authorization with MCP authorization unless clients enforce a structured policy.

standards_alignment.name: Model Context Protocol Elicitation
standards_alignment.coverage: Form-mode and URL-mode elicitation, sensitive data handling, user consent, decline/cancel actions, URL safe handling, user identity binding, statefulness, and external OAuth/payment/API-key flow separation.

standards_alignment.name: Model Context Protocol Authorization
standards_alignment.coverage: OAuth 2.1 alignment, protected resource metadata, resource indicators, audience-bound tokens, client identity metadata, scope challenges, and token-passthrough denial for protected MCP servers.

standards_alignment.name: MCP Security Best Practices
standards_alignment.coverage: Confused-deputy prevention, token passthrough avoidance, session safety, user consent, URL and SSRF controls, auditability, and least-privilege tool access.

standards_alignment.name: The 2026 MCP Roadmap
standards_alignment.coverage: MCP enterprise readiness priorities including audit trails, SSO-integrated authorization, gateway behavior, configuration portability, stateful sessions, and production deployment gaps.

standards_alignment.name: Tool Annotations as Risk Vocabulary
standards_alignment.coverage: Risk vocabulary for MCP tools, untrusted annotations, open-world external interaction, and session-combination risk that informs elicitation preflight and URL-mode controls.

standards_alignment.name: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026
standards_alignment.coverage: Agent goal hijack, tool misuse, identity and privilege abuse, context poisoning, insecure inter-agent communication, human-agent trust exploitation, cascading failures, and rogue agent containment.

standards_alignment.name: Designing AI agents to resist prompt injection
standards_alignment.coverage: Prompt injection as social engineering, source-to-sink analysis, dangerous actions, sensitive data transmission, URL confirmation, and constraining impact even when manipulation succeeds.

standards_alignment.name: Mitigating the risk of prompt injections in browser use
standards_alignment.coverage: Untrusted web content, browser-agent prompt injection, adversarial context, red teaming, classifier guardrails, and safeguards around agents that browse and act for users.

standards_alignment.name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile
standards_alignment.coverage: Governance, data provenance, third-party component risk, monitoring, measurement, lifecycle risk treatment, and operational controls for deployed generative AI systems.

standards_alignment.name: AI Data Security: Best Practices for Securing Data Used to Train and Operate AI Systems
standards_alignment.coverage: Data confidentiality, integrity, provenance, access control, monitoring, third-party risk, and protection of sensitive and mission-critical data used by AI-enabled systems.

control_checks.control_reason: Servers must not send elicitation requests for modes the client did not declare.
The user must know which MCP server is requesting information before accepting, declining, or canceling.
Elicitation is safe only when users can decline or cancel without the agent silently treating non-response as consent.
Form-mode submissions should be reviewable and editable before user-provided data returns to the MCP server.
Passwords, API keys, tokens, private keys, payment credentials, and session cookies must not be requested through form mode.
Sensitive interactions belong in URL mode so credentials and payments never pass through the LLM context or MCP client.
Non-development URL-mode interactions should use HTTPS so user inputs are not exposed in transit.
URL-mode requests need domain review to reduce phishing, subdomain spoofing, and malicious redirect risk.
Pre-authenticated URLs can impersonate users if exposed to a malicious client or logged in the wrong place.
URLs sent through MCP must not embed credentials, tokens, PII, payment data, or other sensitive values.
Clients must not open URL-mode interactions without explicit consent after presenting the target URL.
URL previews and metadata fetches can leak tokens, identifiers, or intent to an untrusted destination.
Third-party credentials obtained through URL mode must never transit the MCP client or be forwarded as token passthrough.
External OAuth via URL mode is separate from MCP authorization and must not be treated as permission for the MCP client itself.
Elicitation state should be bound to the user identity, not only an MCP session identifier.
Each prompt needs a stable ID so completion notifications, retries, receipts, and incident review bind to the original request.
URL completion notifications must only satisfy the original client and elicitation ID.
Repeated prompts can become social engineering pressure, especially when an agent sees untrusted content.
Elicitation requests need receipt evidence for buyer review, incident response, and user trust investigations.

elicitation_profiles.title: Display Name and Preferences Form
elicitation_profiles.buyer_value: Lets MCP servers collect low-risk user preferences without teaching teams to put credentials or sensitive data into ordinary agent forms.

elicitation_profiles.title: Human Approval Rationale Form
elicitation_profiles.buyer_value: Turns approvals into typed, reviewable receipt evidence instead of vague chat consent that a prompt injection can socially engineer.

elicitation_profiles.title: Third-Party OAuth URL Mode
elicitation_profiles.buyer_value: Supports real enterprise connector onboarding while proving external OAuth never becomes MCP token passthrough or invisible user delegation.

elicitation_profiles.title: Connector API Key URL Mode
elicitation_profiles.buyer_value: Lets the future hosted MCP product collect connector secrets through branded, out-of-band flows without ever exposing raw secrets to the client or model.

elicitation_profiles.title: Payment or Billing URL Mode
elicitation_profiles.buyer_value: Shows investors and acquirers that commerce or billing interactions are possible without making agents silent payment collectors.

elicitation_profiles.title: Credential Form Mode Prohibited
elicitation_profiles.buyer_value: Creates a crisp fail-closed rule: if a form asks for secrets, the client blocks it and records why.

elicitation_profiles.title: Untrusted URL Mode Prohibited
elicitation_profiles.buyer_value: Prevents prompt-injection-controlled content from turning MCP URL elicitation into an enterprise phishing primitive.

workflow_rules.title: MCP Connector Intake Scanner
workflow_rules.evidence_expectation: Connector intake can request out-of-band OAuth or secret setup only through URL mode with domain, identity, and receipt evidence.

workflow_rules.title: Agentic Action Runtime
workflow_rules.evidence_expectation: High-impact action approval must be typed, reviewable, and correlated to the exact run and action envelope.

workflow_rules.title: Agentic App Intake Gate
workflow_rules.evidence_expectation: New agentic apps must prove which elicitation modes they use, what data classes they request, and which URLs users may be sent to.

workflow_rules.title: Browser Agent Workspace Boundary
workflow_rules.evidence_expectation: Browser-agent sessions must block untrusted URL prompts and form-secret requests because the page itself may be adversarial.

buyer_due_diligence_questions.question: Can MCP servers request secrets, payment data, or tokens through ordinary form prompts?
buyer_due_diligence_questions.answer_contract: Return form-mode deny rules, sensitive data classes, profile decisions, source references, and runtime evaluator outcomes.

buyer_due_diligence_questions.question: What prevents an MCP server or prompt-injection-controlled tool result from sending users to a malicious URL?
buyer_due_diligence_questions.answer_contract: Return URL-mode safe handling rules, allowlisted domains, no-prefetch controls, consent requirements, and deny decisions for untrusted URLs.

buyer_due_diligence_questions.question: How does external OAuth through URL mode differ from MCP authorization?
buyer_due_diligence_questions.answer_contract: Return the authorization separation controls, source references, required hashes, and token-passthrough deny behavior.

buyer_due_diligence_questions.question: Can a buyer review who asked the user for information, why, and what decision was made?
buyer_due_diligence_questions.answer_contract: Return required receipt fields, workflow mappings, generated pack hashes, and the correlation fields needed for enterprise review.

enterprise_adoption_packet.board_level_claim: SecurityRecipes can make MCP user prompts safe enough for production by distinguishing low-risk forms from sensitive URL-mode flows and by denying secret collection through the LLM or MCP client.
default_questions_answered: Which MCP elicitation modes are allowed by workflow?
Which data classes must use URL mode instead of form mode?
Which URL checks happen before a user is sent out of band?
Which external authorization flows are separate from MCP authorization?
Which receipt fields prove consent, identity, URL, and decision evidence?
enterprise_adoption_packet.recommended_first_use: Apply this pack during remote MCP rollout, connector intake, AI app launch review, browser-agent deployment, and procurement review for any MCP server that can ask users for information.
enterprise_adoption_packet.sales_motion: Lead with open elicitation rules, then sell hosted MCP prompt and URL safety enforcement, customer domain allowlists, consent receipts, phishing telemetry, and connector setup flows.
